Turns out URLs aren't magic! Behind DNS is a whole lot of lookup work
"Everyone, starting today let's try using Google to search for info!" the teacher said in front of the class.
Actually, when I first started using computers, I didn't use Google — I used
. Yahoo! was one of the most-used search engines in Taiwan back then.
"Open your browser, type G...o...o...g...l...e..., then .c...o...m, and press Enter!" the teacher said.
I still remember Google's homepage being super minimalist and clean — you couldn't tell what services they offered.
Compared to Yahoo! Kimo's site at the time, Google felt way less impressive.
So I still mostly used Yahoo back then. So when did I switch to Google?
I think it was around middle school. My classmates started using Gmail one by one. To keep up, I signed up for Gmail too, and gradually got used to Google Search.
The teacher had said something memorable:
"Google will probably become the main search engine for everyone in the future, so you really should learn how to use it."
That teacher's foresight was sharp. If I'd had money to buy stocks back then, I'd be rich now (lol).
Let me use a real-life example to make the abstract Domain concept easier.
Imagine you're mailing something or trying to find a place. You'd usually describe it like:
"123 Songshan Rd, Xinyi District, Taipei City"
That's a familiar address — easy to remember, easy to describe.
But in reality, that address gets converted into specific coordinates for map services, like:
Those coordinates pin the location precisely, but can you remember them? I definitely can't. Our brains prefer meaningful info over long meaningless numbers.
Same with the internet. Take Google — we know its Domain is
, but do you know its IP?
Most people don't. So here's a trick to look up a Domain's IP.
Open your terminal (Command Prompt) and type:
This command just queries the domain's IP address — won't affect your computer.

Server: 192.168.50.1
Address: 192.168.50.1#53

Non-authoritative answer:
Name: www.google.com
Address: 142.250.66.68
The Address you see might not be
— Google has many nodes worldwide and returns different IPs based on your location. "Nodes" means Google's servers in different regions.
How to read this? Here's a table:
Your computer's DNS server, usually your home router's IP.
The IP info isn't from Google's authoritative DNS, but from a relay or cache server.
— one of Google's nodes (may vary by region).
So why do we need Domain? Imagine Google's IP was a hard-to-remember string like:
How would you remember it? Memorize the string? Or with IPv6:
Can you memorize that? Most people can't — definitely not me. That's why we need Domain to help with memory.
From this, we can see what a good domain has:
Related to the brand or service

Classic examples: Google, Facebook, YouTube. Good domains matter for brand-building and marketing, which is also why quality domains are hard to get and pricey — sometimes thousands or tens of thousands of dollars a year. But for brands, the image and marketing value is usually worth it.
Because of this, domain names follow "
". If the domain you want is already taken, you can't reuse it — pick another or try negotiating with the current owner.
This setup led to a particular business behavior: "
" (also called "Cybersquatters", which I'll just call domain squatters below).
Domain squatters register large numbers of domain names, then resell them at high prices to people who actually need them. They typically grab "
" domains, sit on them, or use them for parking-page ads, waiting for someone to pay up.
, because domain names themselves don't have copyright or trademark protection. As long as you can pay, you can register any domain you want.
A side note on buying — domains are bought through "
Google Domains (ended in 2023, migrated to Squarespace)

These registrars apply for domain names through ICANN (Internet Corporation for Assigned Names and Numbers) and resell them to end users. Note: buying a domain doesn't give you ownership — it gives you

A domain can be registered for up to 10 years at a time, with continuous renewal allowed — so as long as you keep renewing, you can keep the domain. If you don't renew, the domain gets released and someone else can re-register it. That's when
might grab it and resell to the original user at a high price.
If you're considering buying your own domain, here are quick pointers:
Domains aren't one-off purchases — usage is
at a time; renewal required after.
→ finally released for others to buy.
Watch the pricing model — sometimes first year is cheap and renewal is expensive (e.g., $10 first year, $20+ renewal).
A good domain: memorable, brand/service-related, short, easy to type, hard to misspell.
If your target domain is registered, negotiate with the owner — usually via
The ending part of a domain, e.g.,
. Different TLDs have very different pricing, renewal, and availability.
to avoid your personal info (name, phone, email) being public.
After registration, you need to set DNS records (A, CNAME, MX, TXT...) to make the site and email work.
" — it's really important for personal data protection.

Before buying, confirm the registrar offers WHOIS privacy protection. This service hides your personal info from the public WHOIS database. Without it, your name, phone, email, etc. are public — anyone can look them up. Very unsafe for personal privacy.
"Why does WHOIS expose personal info? What gets exposed?" Probably what you're wondering.
Short answer: this comes from the
. ICANN (Internet Corporation for Assigned Names and Numbers) explicitly requires registrars to provide registrant info, for transparency and abuse prevention (scams, spam).
Some regions, due to GDPR, may auto-hide WHOIS data (e.g., .eu domains), but it doesn't apply to all TLDs — .com, .tw, etc. may still expose personal info.

Domain Name: example.com
Registrar: XYZ Registrar, Inc.
Registrant Name: Ray Chen
Registrant Organization: 個人
Registrant Street: Taipei City, Xinyi District, Songshan Rd. 123
Registrant Phone: +886.912345678
Registrant Email: ray@example.com
Registrant Street: Taipei City, Xinyi District, Songshan Rd. 123
Because your personal data is so important, I recommend buying WHOIS privacy protection. Some related articles I've written:
From zero — how to buy a domain (Google Domain) and set it as your blog URL
Google Domains ended — using Cloudflare to set up domains and deploy sites
Hope these help you avoid pitfalls.
"Your computer's DNS server is usually your home router's IP."
Yes — you use it every day, you just don't know it. But this isn't a real DNS server.
DNS (Domain Name System) is like the internet's phone book — it converts human-friendly URLs (like Google.com) into the IPs computers understand. Critical infrastructure for the whole internet.
Without DNS, you'd have to use long number strings to visit sites — like having to use coordinates to order delivery.
Your home router isn't a real DNS server — it's a DNS relay.
When you type a Domain into the browser and submit, your router sends the Domain to a real DNS server. The DNS server returns the IP to the router, and the router returns the IP to your computer.
". With this design, all devices at home (computers, phones, tablets) only need to query the router — no per-device DNS setup — making browsing faster.
This design also lets the router cache DNS, avoiding repeated queries to the DNS server, speeding things up.
Browser → Router DNS → ISP DNS → Root DNS → TLD DNS → Authoritative DNS → return IP
". Every URL you type might go through this whole chain.
What was the first search engine you ever used?
Apparently the answer to this can roughly tell your age range XD.

URLs aren't magic. Starting from nslookup, this post walks through Domain vs. IP, DNS forwarding / caching / resolution paths, and the essentials of domain buying, renewal, TLDs, and WHOIS privacy — so the "type a URL → get an IP" process becomes clearer.
